The moon's cycles determine the Jewish festival calendar to this day precisely because of this Genesis 1:14 assignment.

ולימים.explanation:
The form *ולימים (ûlĕyāmîm)* consists of *ו (wĕ)* (and) + *ל (lĕ)* (for) + *ימים (yāmîm)* (days, plural of יום). BDB: *ימים (yāmîm)* — days. Strong's H3117.

The luminaries mark not only sacred seasons (*מועדים*) but also ordinary days (*ימים*). The sun's daily cycle establishes the basic unit of time: one sunrise to the next is one day. The moon's monthly cycle and the sun's yearly cycle follow. The progression from larger to smaller units — signs, seasons, days, years — or perhaps from qualitative to quantitative — establishes a complete temporal framework governed by created celestial objects.

ושנים.explanation:
The form *ושנים (wĕšānîm)* consists of *ו (wĕ)* (and) + *שנים (šānîm)* (years, plural of שנה). BDB: *שנה (šānāh)* — year; the cycle of the seasons, approximately 365 days. Strong's H8141 documents approximately 877 occurrences.

The root *שנה (šānāh)* also means to repeat, to do again — a year is the completed repetition of the seasonal cycle. The luminaries carry the responsibility for marking years because the sun's annual cycle through the zodiac (in ancient cosmology) defines the year. The assignment of signs, seasons, days, and years to the luminaries in verse 14 — and confirmed in verse 18 — makes the celestial bodies into a complete temporal instrument. Creation is not just ordered in space (firmament separating waters) but in time (luminaries governing days, months, seasons, years).

למאורות.explanation:
The form *למאורות (lim·ʾōrōṯ)* is *ל (lĕ)* (for/as) + *מאורות (mĕʾōrōṯ)* (luminaries, plural). In verse 15, *והיו למאורות (wĕhāyū lim·ʾōrōṯ)* — and let them be as/for lights — the *ל (lĕ)* before *מאורות* is the lamed of predication or purpose: the things in the sky are to serve as luminaries. BDB: the noun *מאור (māʾôr)* with *ל (lĕ)* denoting functional designation. Strong's H3974.

The verse restates the luminaries' function from verse 14 in condensed form, preparing for the fulfillment formula *ויהי כן (wayhî kên)*. The repetition is rhetorical emphasis: the purpose of the luminaries is not assumed but stated, restated, and then confirmed. The created order is intentional and purposive — every element has an assigned function.

להאיר.explanation:
The form *להאיר (lĕhāʾîr)* is the Hiphil infinitive construct of the root *אור (ʾôr)* — to cause to shine, to give light, to illuminate. BDB: Hiphil of *אור (ʾôr)* — to cause to shine, to illuminate. Strong's H216.

The Hiphil (causative) stem marks the luminaries as instruments that cause light to reach the earth — they are conduits, not sources. The light exists (created in verse 3); the luminaries are assigned to channel it earthward. *להאיר על הארץ (lĕhāʾîr ʿal hāʾāreṣ)* — to give light upon the earth — specifies the directionality: from sky to earth. Creation is a structured system of directed illumination, not merely scattered brightness. The same Hiphil root appears in verse 17 confirming the fulfillment, and the consonance between command and fulfillment is lexically exact.

המאורות.explanation:
The definite form *המאורות (hammĕʾōrōṯ)* — the luminaries — with the definite article. By verse 16, the luminaries have been established as commanded objects (v.14), named by their function (מאורות, v.14–15), and are now being made. The article marks them as the specific lights that were commanded, now being brought into existence with a making act. BDB: *מאור (māʾôr)* with article. Strong's H3974.

The double appearance of *המאורות הגדלים (hammĕʾōrōṯ haggĕḏōlîm)* — the great luminaries — as the overarching group, followed by the distinction between *המאור הגדל (hammāʾôr haggāḏōl)* (the greater light) and *המאור הקטן (hammāʾôr haqqāṭōn)* (the lesser light), is a rhetorical move from general to specific. The group is introduced, then each member is assigned its particular governance function.

הגדלים.explanation:
The form *הגדלים (haggĕḏōlîm)* is the definite masculine plural adjective great — the great ones. BDB: *גדול (gāḏôl)* — great, large, important; plural *גדולים (gĕḏōlîm)*. Strong's H1419 documents approximately 526 occurrences.

The root *גדל (gāḏal)* means to be great, to grow great, to magnify. In verse 16, *המאורות הגדלים (hammĕʾōrōṯ haggĕḏōlîm)* — the great lights — uses the plural adjective to describe the two lights as a pair. Then the superlative distinction is made: one is the greater (*הגדל, haggāḏōl*) and one the lesser (*הקטן, haqqāṭōn*). The comparative degree is expressed through juxtaposition, not a morphological comparative suffix — Biblical Hebrew often compares by pairing rather than suffixing.

הגדל.explanation:
The definite singular adjective *הגדל (haggāḏōl)* — the great one, the greater — modifying *המאור (hammāʾôr)* (the light). In context, it refers to the sun as the greater luminary, though the text never names it as such. BDB: *גדול (gāḏôl)* — great, large. Strong's H1419.

The avoidance of the sun's name (*שמש, šemeš*) and the moon's name (*ירח, yārêaḥ*) in Genesis 1:16 is deliberate. *Šemeš* and *Yārêaḥ* are names of solar and lunar deities in ancient Semitic religions. Genesis 1 describes the sun and moon by their functional attributes — greater and lesser light — not by their divine names. The demythologization of the celestial bodies is embedded in the choice of vocabulary.

לממשלת.explanation:
The form *לממשלת (lĕmemšeleṯ)* is *ל (lĕ)* (for) + *ממשלת (memšeleṯ)* — dominion, rule, governance. BDB: *ממשלה (memšālāh)* — dominion, rule, realm; from the root *משל (māšal)* — to rule, to govern, to have dominion. Strong's H4475.

The language of *ממשלת (memšeleṯ)* — dominion — frames the luminaries' function in royal terms. The sun rules (*ממשלת היום*, dominion of the day); the moon rules (*ממשלת הלילה*, dominion of the night). This is governance language: the luminaries hold office. They do not merely illuminate; they govern the time divisions of the created order. The same root *משל* appears in verse 18 (*למשל*, to rule) describing the luminaries' ongoing function, and in verse 28 (*ורדו*, have dominion) describing humanity's mandate.

הקטן.explanation:
The definite singular adjective *הקטן (haqqāṭōn)* — the small one, the lesser — describes the moon as the lesser luminary compared to *הגדל (haggāḏōl)* (the greater). BDB: *קטן (qāṭān)* — small, little, insignificant; opposite of גדול. Strong's H6996.

The pairing *הגדל / הקטן* — greater / lesser — makes a relative distinction without denigrating the lesser. The moon is not bad or unimportant; it is lesser in magnitude. Its assignment — to rule the night — is no less specific than the sun's. The paired designations — one greater, one lesser — balance the creation: day has its governor, night has its governor, and the two together cover all waking and sleeping time. *הקטן* also appears in social contexts as the younger/lesser sibling, giving it a familial resonance.

הכוכבים.explanation:
The noun *כוכבים (kôḵāḇîm)* — stars — with the definite article. BDB: *כוכב (kôḵāḇ)* — star; plural *כוכבים (kôḵāḇîm)*. Strong's H3556 documents approximately 37 occurrences.

The addition *ואת הכוכבים (wĕʾêṯ hakkôḵāḇîm)* — and the stars — in verse 16 is strikingly brief. In an ancient world where stars were individually named and religiously venerated (the Babylonian mul.APIN star catalog lists hundreds of named stars), Genesis 1:16 mentions the stars in a dependent clause, almost parenthetically. The theological message is pointed: the stars — objects of elaborate astral religion across the ancient Near East — are included in divine creation alongside the two great lights, with no further comment. Their existence, not their identity or divinity, is the point.

ויתן.gloss: and he set, and he gave
ויתן.explanation:
The form *ויתן (wayyittên)* is the Qal wayyiqtol 3ms of the root *נתן (nāṯan)* — and he gave, and he set, and he placed. BDB: *נתן (nāṯan)* — to give, to set, to place, to appoint; the most versatile Hebrew verb for granting or assigning. Strong's H5414 documents approximately 2,000 occurrences.

In verse 17, *ויתן אתם אלהים ברקיע השמים (wayyittên ʾōṯām ʾĕlōhîm birqîaʿ haššāmāyim)* — God set them in the expanse of the heavens. The verb *נתן (nāṯan)* describes the act of placement — God assigns the luminaries to their cosmic positions. This is appointment language: God does not merely create the luminaries and leave them floating; he gives them a position. *ויתן* thus completes the creative act: speak (v.14), make (v.16), place (v.17).

אתם.explanation:
The form *אתם (ʾōṯām)* is the direct object pronoun them — the accusative 3mp pronoun. It is the combination of the direct object marker *את (ʾêṯ)* with the 3mp suffix *ם (ām)*: literally, them (as direct object). BDB: pronominal suffix with accusative marker. Strong's H853+H240.

In verse 17, *ויתן אתם אלהים (wayyittên ʾōṯām ʾĕlōhîm)* — God set them — the *אתם (ʾōṯām)* refers to the luminaries just described. The pronoun replaces repetition: instead of repeating המאורות (the luminaries), the text uses the pronoun to maintain narrative flow. The same form will appear in verse 22 (*ויברך אתם, wayyĕḇāreḵ ʾōṯām*, he blessed them) and verse 28 (*ויברך אתם, wayyĕḇāreḵ ʾōṯām*, he blessed them again, of humans).

ולמשל.explanation:
The form *ולמשל (wĕlimšōl)* is *ו (wĕ)* (and) + *ל (lĕ)* (infinitive marker) + the Qal infinitive construct of the root *משל (māšal)* — and to rule, and to exercise dominion. BDB: *משל (māšal)* — to rule, to govern, to have dominion; used of kings, celestial bodies, and the strong over the weak. Strong's H4910 documents approximately 81 occurrences.

In verse 18, *ולמשל ביום ובלילה (wĕlimšōl bayyôm ûḇallaylāh)* — and to rule over the day and over the night — the luminaries' governance function is restated as an infinitive of purpose: they were placed to rule. The same root *משל* underlies *ממשלת (memšeleṯ)* (dominion) in verse 16. Governance is the luminaries' defining vocation: they were made for it, placed for it, and described twice as executing it.

ביום.gloss: in the day, on the day
ביום.explanation:
The form *ביום (bĕyôm)* is the preposition *ב (bĕ)* — in, on — + *יום (yôm)* — day. Together: on the day, in the day, when. BDB: *יום (yôm)* — day, period of time, moment. Strong's H3117.

*bĕyôm* in 2:17 introduces the conditional time clause: *bĕyôm ʾăḵālĕḵā mimmenū* — on the day that you eat of it [you shall die]. The *bĕyôm* construction is often used as a temporal conjunction — when/at the time that. This is important for the interpretation of the death warning: the human does not die on the literal calendar day of eating (Genesis 3 narrates ongoing life after the fall). Ancient and modern interpreters have read *bĕyôm* as: (1) immediately that day (literal), requiring either a meaning of immediate spiritual death or an extended calendar; (2) at the moment when — i.e., mortality becomes your condition from that point; or (3) as a beginning of the death process.

ובלילה.explanation:
The form *ובלילה (ûḇallaylāh)* is *ו (wĕ)* (and) + *ב (bĕ)* (in) + *לילה (lāyĕlāh)* (night) with the article, which assimilates: and in the night, and during/over the night. BDB: *לילה (lāyĕlāh)* with *ב (bĕ)* preposition. Strong's H3915.

The parallel construction *ביום ובלילה (bayyôm ûḇallaylāh)* — during the day and during the night — covers the full temporal range. Together, day and night account for all time. By assigning one luminary to each, the text ensures that no temporal domain is ungoverned. This parallels the spatial governance of firmament (separating waters) with temporal governance of luminaries (separating day and night): creation is governed in its spatial and temporal dimensions.

ולהבדיל.explanation:
The form *ולהבדיל (ûlĕhaḇdîl)* is *ו (wĕ)* (and) + *ל (lĕ)* (infinitive marker) + Hiphil infinitive of *בדל (bādal)* — and to (cause to) separate. The same root as *להבדיל (lĕhaḇdîl)* in verse 14. BDB: Hiphil infinitive of *בדל (bādal)*. Strong's H914.

The sixth occurrence of *בדל* in Genesis 1 here confirms the ongoing, delegated character of separation. God separated in verse 4; the luminaries are assigned to continue the separation in verses 14, 18. The act of separation that began the ordering of creation is built into the structure of the luminaries' function. Every day and night cycle is a renewed act of the primordial separation. The grammar of creation — distinction, boundary, order — is maintained by the celestial clockwork.

רביעי.explanation:
The ordinal *רביעי (rĕḇîʿî)* means fourth, from the root *ארבע (ʾarbaʿ)* (four). BDB: *רביעי (rĕḇîʿî)* — fourth (ordinal). Strong's H7243. It closes the formula for day four, paralleling שני (second) for day two and שלישי (third) for day three.

Day four pairs structurally with day one in the creation week's architecture: day one creates light; day four creates light-bearers. The pairing (days 1–4, 2–5, 3–6) makes day four the middle point of the two-triad structure. Days one through three establish the domains (light/dark, waters/sky, land/sea); days four through six fill those domains (luminaries, sea creatures and birds, land animals and humans). The ordinal number marks the day's place in this precise six-day framework.

ישרצו.explanation:
The form *ישרצו (yišrĕṣū)* is the Qal jussive 3mp of the root *שרץ (šāraṣ)* — let them swarm, teem, abound. BDB: *שרץ (šāraṣ)* — to swarm, to teem; used of the masses of small, rapid-moving creatures in water. Strong's H8317.

The root *שרץ (šāraṣ)* describes the characteristic motion of water creatures — frantic, teeming, swarming. The jussive mood makes this a divine command addressed to the waters: let them swarm. In Genesis 1:20, *יִשְׁרְצוּ הַמַּיִם שֶׁרֶץ (yišrĕṣū hammāyim šereṣ)* — let the waters swarm with swarms — is a cognate accusative: the verb and its object are from the same root, intensifying the swarming. The same root *שרץ* appears in the fulfillment (v.21: *שרצו המים*, the waters swarmed), confirming exact compliance.

שרץ.explanation:
The noun *שרץ (šereṣ)* means a swarm of creatures, swarming things — small, rapidly moving creatures that move in groups. BDB: *שרץ (šereṣ)* — swarm, teeming creatures; specifically water and land creatures characterized by rapid, swarming movement. Strong's H8318 documents 15 occurrences.

In Genesis 1:20, *שרץ נפש חיה (šereṣ nep̄eš ḥayyāh)* — swarms of living creatures — identifies the swarming things as *נפש חיה (nep̄eš ḥayyāh)* — living souls. Small sea creatures at the bottom of the creation's living hierarchy share the same categorization as humanity in one respect: they are all *נפש חיה*. The same phrase applies to fish, birds, land animals, and in Genesis 2:7, to Adam when God breathes life into him.

נפש.explanation:
The noun *נפש (nep̄eš)* is one of the most theologically and semantically rich words in the Hebrew Bible. BDB defines its range as: soul, living being, life, self, desire, appetite, emotion, passion — the inner animating quality of a living thing. Strong's H5315 documents approximately 754 occurrences.

In Genesis 1, *נפש (nep̄eš)* first appears in the compound *נפש חיה (nep̄eš ḥayyāh)* — living soul/creature — applied to water creatures (v.20, 21), land creatures (v.24), and in Genesis 2:7, explicitly to the human formed from dust when God breathes into him. The use of *נפש ḥayyāh* for both animals and humans (2:7) has made it a central term in debates about the nature of the soul. *Nep̄eš* refers not to an immaterial component separate from the body but to the whole living being as an experiencing, desiring, animated entity.

חיה.explanation:
The adjective-noun *חיה (ḥayyāh)* means living, alive, full of life — and as a noun, living creature, animal. BDB: *חי (ḥay)* — living; *חיה (ḥayyāh)* — living creature, animal, wild beast. Strong's H2416 documents approximately 266 occurrences.

In Genesis 1, *חיה (ḥayyāh)* appears both as an adjective in *נפש חיה (nep̄eš ḥayyāh)* (living soul/creature) and as a noun *חית הארץ (ḥayaṯ hāʾāreṣ)* (wild animals of the earth). The root *חיה (ḥāyāh)* is related to *חי (ḥay)* (life) and the verb *חיה (ḥāyāh)* (to live). Life as movement, animation, and vitality runs through the entire word family. In Genesis 1:20 and 21, the first occurrences of *נפש חיה* establish the category of animate life as a class distinct from plants and inert matter.

ועוף.explanation:
The noun *עוף (ʿôp̄)* means flying creatures, birds — any creature that flies. BDB: *עוף (ʿôp̄)* — bird, flying creature; from the root *עוף (ʿûp̄)* — to fly. Strong's H5775 documents approximately 71 occurrences. The form *ועוף (wĕʿôp̄)* adds the conjunctive *ו (wĕ)* — and birds.

The word *עוף (ʿôp̄)* covers all flying creatures, not just domesticated birds. It includes wild birds, bats, and insects in some contexts. BDB notes that the lexeme encompasses the entire category of things that fly. In verse 20, *ועוף יעופף על הארץ (wĕʿôp̄ yĕʿôp̄êp̄ ʿal hāʾāreṣ)* — and birds shall fly above the earth — pairs the noun *עוף* with the cognate verb *עוף* in an intensive participle form, once again using cognate accusative for rhetorical emphasis.

יעופף.explanation:
The form *יעופף (yĕʿôp̄êp̄)* is the Piel imperfect 3ms of the root *עוף (ʿûp̄)* — to fly, to soar. BDB: Piel of *עוף (ʿûp̄)* — intensive or frequentative: to fly rapidly, to flutter, to soar. Strong's H5774. The Piel stem intensifies the flying action; combined with the pairing of noun *עוף (ʿôp̄)* and verb *יעופף*, the text achieves a vivid, almost onomatopoeic effect.

The letter doubling in the Piel root (*עוף → יעופף*) mirrors the doubled wing-beat of flying. The word sounds like what it describes. This is part of Genesis 1's systematic use of sound and meaning together — *תהו ובהו (ṯōhû wāḇōhû)* (formlessness and void) sounds hollow; *ישרצו...שרץ (yišrĕṣū...šereṣ)* sounds frantic and swarming; *יעופף (yĕʿôp̄êp̄)* sounds like flight. Hebrew creation language is aurally evocative.

ויברא.explanation:
The form *ויברא (wayyiḇrāʾ)* is the Qal wayyiqtol 3ms of the root *ברא (bārāʾ)* — and he created. This is the fourth use of the unique verb *ברא (bārāʾ)* in Genesis 1 (appearing in 1:1, 1:21, and three times in 1:27). BDB: *ברא (bārāʾ)* — to create; used exclusively with God as subject in the Qal. Strong's H1254.

The reappearance of *ויברא (wayyiḇrāʾ)* in verse 21 marks the creation of the first animate living creatures as a qualitatively significant act — distinct from the making (*ויעש*) of the firmament and luminaries. The unique verb signals that animate life is not merely repositioned matter but a new category of reality. The same verb will appear three times in verse 27 for the creation of humanity, further intensifying the signal. *ברא (bārāʾ)* marks the theological apex acts of creation.

התנינם.explanation:
The noun *תנינים (tannînîm)* — great sea monsters, sea creatures — has both a mythological and a natural referent. BDB: *תנין (tannîn)* — sea serpent, sea monster, dragon; used of large sea creatures and of the chaos dragon in mythological contexts. Strong's H8577 documents 14 occurrences.

Elsewhere in the Hebrew Bible, *תנין (tannîn)* refers to the mythological chaos creature Leviathan (Ps 74:13, Isa 51:9), the dragon of chaos that only God can subdue. In Genesis 1:21, the tannînim are simply created objects — *ויברא אלהים את התנינם הגדלים (wayyiḇrāʾ ʾĕlōhîm ʾeṯ hattannînîm haggĕḏōlîm)* — God created the great sea creatures. The chaos monster becomes a creature. The contrast with Babylonian and Canaanite myths where the sea monster must be violently defeated to create the world is stark. Genesis 1 creates without conflict.

הרמשת.explanation:
The form *הרמשת (hārōmeśeṯ)* is the Qal feminine participle of the root *רמש (rāmaś)* with the article — the creeping one, that which creeps. BDB: *רמש (rāmaś)* — to creep, to crawl, to move along the ground; used of all crawling or creeping creatures. Strong's H7430.

The participial form describes the characteristic movement of a class of creatures: those that move by creeping. In verse 21, *כל נפש החיה הרמשת אשר שרצו המים (kol nep̄eš haḥayyāh hārōmeśeṯ ʾăšer šārĕṣū hammāyim)* — every living soul that creeps with which the waters abounded — the feminine participle *הרמשת* refers to the creeping water creatures. The same root *רמש* appears as a noun *רמש (remeś)* in verse 24 for land-creeping things, showing a single root covers both aquatic and land-based crawling motion.

שרצו.explanation:
The form *שרצו (šārĕṣū)* is the Qal perfect 3mp of the root *שרץ (šāraṣ)* — they swarmed, they teemed. The perfect form here describes the action as completed from the narrative perspective: the waters swarmed (as a result of God's creative command). BDB: Qal perfect — the waters produced swarms. Strong's H8317.

In verse 21, *שרצו המים (šārĕṣū hammāyim)* — the waters swarmed — is the fulfilled form of *ישרצו המים (yišrĕṣū hammāyim)* (let the waters swarm, v.20). Command (jussive) and fulfillment (perfect) use the same root, confirming exact correspondence. The waters that were commanded to swarm did in fact swarm. The perfect tense in narrative sequence marks completed action viewed retrospectively — a characteristic function of the Qal perfect in Biblical Hebrew.

למינהם.explanation:
The form *למינהם (lĕmînêhem)* is *ל (lĕ)* (according to) + *מין (mîn)* (kind) + *הם (hem)* (3mp possessive suffix) — according to their kinds. This is the 3rd masculine plural possessive form, used when multiple species are described as each reproducing after its own kind. BDB: *מין (mîn)* — species, kind. Strong's H4327.

In verse 21, *למינהם (lĕmînêhem)* applies to the water creatures (they swarmed according to their kinds), while in the preceding clause *למינהו (lĕmînêhū)* applies to birds (each according to its own kind). The variation between singular possessive (*הו*) and plural possessive (*הם*) tracks grammatical agreement: single species reference gets singular suffix; collective reference to multiple species gets plural. The pattern-repetition of lemîn across the creation week establishes kind-faithfulness as the foundational principle of biological continuity.

כנף.explanation:
The noun *כנף (kānāp̄)* means wing — of a bird, or by extension edge, border, extremity of a garment or land. BDB: *כנף (kānāp̄)* — wing; also corner, edge, extremity; used of birds' wings, corners of garments, and edges of the earth. Strong's H3671 documents approximately 108 occurrences.

In Genesis 1:21, *כל עוף כנף (kol ʿôp̄ kānāp̄)* — every winged bird — the construct *כנף (kānāp̄)* (of wing) specifies the defining characteristic of birds: they have wings. This is taxonomic language — wings are the categorizing feature that places a creature in the bird class. The same noun appears in Psalm 17:8 (shadow of your wings) and Ruth 2:12 (under whose wings you have come for refuge), where the wing becomes a metaphor for divine protection — an extension of the concrete physical wing of verse 21.

ויברך.explanation:
The form *ויברך (wayyĕḇāreḵ)* is the Piel wayyiqtol 3ms of the root *ברך (bāraḵ)* — and he blessed. BDB: Piel of *ברך (bāraḵ)* — to bless; to confer prosperity, vitality, and reproductive capacity. The Piel of *ברך* is the standard form for the act of blessing — whether human-to-human or divine-to-creature. Strong's H1288 documents approximately 327 occurrences of the Piel.

The first divine blessing in the Bible (v.22) is directed at living creatures, not at humans. Blessing in Genesis 1 is a speech act that confers productive capacity: the fish and birds are blessed with the capacity to fill the waters and multiply. The same blessing formula (*ויברך אתם אלהים*, and God blessed them) appears in verse 28 for humans, extending the creature blessing to its climactic recipient. Blessing is the divine gift of fruitful vitality.

לאמר.explanation:
The form *לאמר (lêmōr)* is the preposition *ל (lĕ)* + Qal Infinitive Construct of *אמר (ʾāmar)* — to say. As a formulaic phrase: saying, as follows. BDB: *לאמר* — saying — a standard formula introducing direct speech after verbs of speaking or commanding. Strong's H559.

*לאמר (lêmōr)* appears hundreds of times in the Hebrew Bible as a quotation marker: God said [to them] *lêmōr*, saying, and then the quoted speech follows. It appears after *wayĕṣaw (commanded)* in 2:16, introducing the garden command as direct divine speech. The formula emphasizes the direct, oral quality of divine communication: God does not issues abstract decrees but speaks words — *lêmōr* — that can be quoted back. Moses uses the same formula throughout Deuteronomy when recounting God's words to Israel.

פרו.explanation:
The form *פרו (pĕrū)* is the Qal imperative 2mp of the root *פרה (pārāh)* — be fruitful! The Qal imperative plural addresses a group. BDB: *פרה (pārāh)* — to bear fruit, to be fruitful. Strong's H6509.

The root *פרה (pārāh)* is the verb form of *פרי (pĕrî)* (fruit). Just as trees bear fruit, creatures are commanded to bear fruit — to reproduce. The imagery is botanical applied to biology: animals and humans are to do what trees do, to produce offspring as trees produce fruit. This verbal echo connects day three's vegetation (fruit trees, v.11–12) with day five's creatures (v.22) and day six's humans (v.28). The creation week's vocabulary deliberately threads through its parts.

ורבו.explanation:
The form *ורבו (ûrĕḇū)* is *ו (wĕ)* (and) + the Qal imperative 2mp of the root *רבה (rāḇāh)* — and multiply! BDB: *רבה (rāḇāh)* — to be or become many, to multiply, to increase. Strong's H7235 documents approximately 229 occurrences.

The pairing *פרו ורבו (pĕrū ûrĕḇū)* — be fruitful and multiply — is formula-like in the Hebrew Bible, appearing in 1:22, 1:28, 8:17, 9:1, 9:7, 17:20, 28:3, and 35:11. Its repetition across Genesis and Exodus makes it a covenantal refrain: the blessing of reproduction is at the heart of God's purposes for creatures and for Israel. The two verbs are near-synonyms — fruitfulness and multiplication — but their pairing is emphatic: double the command, double the assurance of productive vitality.

ומלאו.explanation:
The form *ומלאו (ûmilʾū)* is *ו (wĕ)* (and) + the Qal imperative 2mp of the root *מלא (māleʾ)* — and fill! BDB: *מלא (māleʾ)* — to be full, to fill, to complete. Strong's H4390 documents approximately 252 occurrences.

The command to fill continues the theme established by the days-of-forming-then-filling structure of Genesis 1. Days one through three create and bound the domains (light/dark, sky/sea, land/sea). Days four through six fill those domains with inhabitants. The command *ומלאו (ûmilʾū)* makes explicit that filling is the creatures' vocation: the created spaces are incomplete without their inhabitants. The cosmos is designed to be inhabited. This creative purpose — a filled, inhabited world — is the goal toward which all the forming acts of the first three days aim.

בימים.explanation:
The form *בימים (bayyammîm)* is the preposition *ב (bĕ)* (in) + *ימים (yammîm)* (seas, plural of ים) with the definite article assimilating: in the seas. BDB: *ים (yām)* — sea, seas. Strong's H3220.

The seas were named in verse 10 — the gathered waters were called *ימים (yammîm)*. Here in verse 22, sea creatures are blessed and commanded to fill those same seas. The verbal echo is precise: the domains created and named in days 1–3 are filled by the creatures blessed in days 4–6. *בימים (bayyammîm)* links back to verse 10's *ימים (yammîm)* and forward to verse 26's *בדגת הים (biḏgaṯ hayyām)* (over the fish of the sea). The seas are established as a domain, filled as a domain, and governed as a domain.

והעוף.explanation:
The form *והעוף (wĕhāʿôp̄)* is *ו (wĕ)* (and) + *ה (hā)* (the) + *עוף (ʿôp̄)* (birds) — and the birds. The definite article makes this a reference to the specific birds just created in verse 20–21. BDB: *עוף (ʿôp̄)* — bird, flying creature. Strong's H5775.

In the blessing formula of verse 22, the sea creatures are addressed first (*ומלאו את המים בימים*, fill the waters in the seas), and then the birds (*והעוף ירב בארץ*, and the birds shall multiply on the earth). Interestingly, the birds are assigned to multiply on the earth (*בארץ*), not in the sky. This may reflect that birds roost, nest, and reproduce on the ground/land rather than in the air. The sky is their flight-domain; the earth is their reproductive domain.

ירב.explanation:
The form *ירב (yireḇ)* is the Qal imperfect 3ms of the root *רבה (rāḇāh)* — it/he shall multiply. The 3ms form agrees with *העוף (hāʿôp̄)* (birds, collective singular) as grammatical subject. BDB: *רבה (rāḇāh)* — to multiply, to increase. Strong's H7235.

While the sea creatures received the imperative *ורבו (ûrĕḇū)* — multiply! (2mp, addressed directly to them), the birds receive the imperfect *ירב (yireḇ)* — shall/let multiply (3ms, spoken about them). This shift from 2nd to 3rd person is subtle but may reflect that the sea creatures are addressed in the command itself (*פרו ורבו*), while the birds' multiplication is described as a predicted result. Alternatively, it is a formulaic variation without deep semantic significance. Either way, the multiplication command extends to both sea and sky creatures.

בארץ.gloss: in the earth, on the earth
בארץ.explanation:
The form *בארץ (bāʾāreṣ)* is the preposition *ב (bĕ)* (in/on) + *ארץ (ʾāreṣ)* (earth) with the definite article assimilating: in/on the earth. BDB: *ארץ (ʾereṣ)* with *ב (bĕ)* preposition. Strong's H776.

This is the locative complement specifying where birds shall multiply. Combined with *בימים (bayyammîm)* (in the seas, for fish), the two prepositional phrases create the complete geographic scope of the day-five blessing: sea and land. *בארץ (bāʾāreṣ)* as the location of bird reproduction is noteworthy — birds multiply on the earth, not in the sky. This reflects biological reality: most birds nest on the ground or in land-based vegetation. The grammar and the biology align.

חמישי.explanation:
The ordinal *חמישי (ḥămîšî)* means fifth, from the root *חמש (ḥāmêš)* (five). BDB: *חמישי (ḥămîšî)* — fifth (ordinal). Strong's H2568. Day five closes with the standard evening-morning formula plus the ordinal number.

Day five pairs with day two: day two separated sky and sea (the dome creating upper and lower zones); day five fills sky and sea with birds and fish. The structural pairing of forming days (1–3) with filling days (4–6) is most visible in this pair: day two makes the sea and sky spaces; day five populates them. The ordinal *חמישי* marks the day's place in this careful architectural sequence.

תוצא.explanation:
The form *תוצא (tôṣêʾ)* is the Hiphil jussive 3fs of the root *יצא (yāṣāʾ)* — let it bring forth. The subject is the earth (*הארץ*, grammatically feminine), addressed by God to produce land animals. BDB: Hiphil of *יצא (yāṣāʾ)* — to cause to come out, to bring forth. Strong's H3318.

The pattern established in verse 11 — the earth actively brings forth vegetation — repeats here for land animals. *תדשא (tadšēʾ) הארץ* (v.11, let the earth bring forth vegetation) parallels *תוצא (tôṣêʾ) הארץ* (v.24, let the earth bring forth living creatures). The earth is not merely acted upon; it participates as a productive agent in creation. This pattern of mediated creation — God commands; the created order acts — runs through the two creative weeks of forming (days 1–3) and filling (days 4–6).

למינה.explanation:
The form *למינה (lĕmînāh)* is *ל (lĕ)* (according to) + *מין (mîn)* (kind) + *ה (āh)* (3fs possessive suffix) — according to its kind (feminine). The feminine suffix agrees with *חיה (ḥayyāh)* and *בהמה (bĕhēmāh)*, both grammatically feminine nouns. BDB: *מין (mîn)*. Strong's H4327.

The shift between *למינה (lĕmînāh)* (with 3fs suffix), *למינו (lĕmînōw)* (3ms suffix), *למינהו (lĕmînêhū)* (long 3ms), and *למינהם (lĕmînêhem)* (3mp) is morphological variation tracking grammatical gender and number agreement with the noun being modified. All forms carry the same meaning: according to its/their kind. The consistent use of the *lemîn* phrase across plant and animal creation (verses 11, 12, 21, 24, 25) makes it the rhythmic refrain of the biological ordering acts.

בהמה.explanation:
The noun *בהמה (bĕhēmāh)* means livestock, domestic animals, cattle — large domesticated quadrupeds. BDB: *בהמה (bĕhēmāh)* — beast, cattle, animal; often distinguished from *חיה (ḥayyāh)* (wild beast) and *רמש (remeś)* (creeping thing). Strong's H929 documents approximately 189 occurrences.

In Genesis 1:24–25, the three categories of land animals are: *חיה (ḥayyāh)* (wild beasts), *בהמה (bĕhēmāh)* (livestock/domestic animals), and *רמש (remeś)* (creeping things). The sequence moves from the wildest to the most domesticated, ending with the smallest crawling creatures. This tripartite categorization of land animals parallels the tripartite categorization of plant life (dešeʾ, ʿēśeḇ, ʿêṣ). Creation is taxonomized as it is made.

ורמש.explanation:
The form *ורמש (wāreme·ś)* is the conjunction *ו (wĕ)* + the noun *רמש (remeś)* — and creeping things. BDB: *רמש (remeś)* — creeping things; creatures that move close to the ground, crawl, or slither. Strong's H7431 documents approximately 17 occurrences.

The noun *רמש (remeś)* is related to the verb *רמש (rāmaś)* — to creep, to move along the ground. Like *שרץ (šereṣ)* for water creatures, *רמש (remeś)* describes a category of creature defined by its mode of movement: ground-level creeping. The category includes reptiles, insects, and small mammals that move close to the ground. In the creation account's taxonomy, *רמש* represents the lowest stratum of land life — literally the creatures closest to the ground.

וחיתו.explanation:
The form *וחיתו (wĕḥaytōw)* is *ו (wĕ)* (and) + *חית (ḥayaṯ)* (the construct form of חיה, wild beast) + *ו (ô)* (construct suffix/possessive). The construct *חית (ḥayaṯ)* + following genitive gives the meaning wild beast of — as in *חית הארץ (ḥayaṯ hāʾāreṣ)* (wild beasts of the earth). BDB: construct of *חיה (ḥayyāh)* — wild animal. Strong's H2416.

In verse 24, *וחיתו ארץ (wĕḥaytōw ʾereṣ)* — and wild animals of the earth — uses the construct chain. The possessive/construct suffix *ו (ô)* on *חית (ḥayaṯ)* links it to the following noun *ארץ (ʾereṣ)*: the wild animals *belonging to* or *of* the earth. This construct relationship emphasizes that wild animals are earth-creatures, derived from and belonging to the land, in the same way that sea creatures belong to the sea.

חית.explanation:
The form *חית (ḥayaṯ)* is the construct form of *חיה (ḥayyāh)* — wild beast of. In Biblical Hebrew, the construct form of a noun appears before a genitive to form a construct chain (noun of noun). *חית הארץ (ḥayaṯ hāʾāreṣ)* — the wild beast of the earth — is a standard construct chain. BDB: *חיה (ḥayyāh)* — living creature, wild animal. Strong's H2416.

The construct form *חית (ḥayaṯ)* versus the absolute form *חיה (ḥayyāh)* illustrates a fundamental feature of Biblical Hebrew morphology: nouns change form when in construct relationship. The construct form is often shorter or has a different vowel pattern. The absolute form stands alone; the construct form reaches forward to the word it governs. *חית הארץ (ḥayaṯ hāʾāreṣ)* — wild beasts of the earth — is one of the most common construct chains in the creation narrative.

הבהמה.explanation:
The definite form *הבהמה (habbĕhēmāh)* — the livestock — with the definite article. By verse 25, the livestock have been established by the command of verse 24 and are now made (*ויעש*). The article marks them as the specific livestock commanded, now existing. BDB: *בהמה (bĕhēmāh)* with article. Strong's H929.

The article on *הבהמה* in verse 25 (fulfillment) versus no article on *בהמה* in verse 24 (command) follows the pattern observed throughout Genesis 1: the indefinite form appears in the command; the definite form appears in the fulfillment. The created thing goes from proposed concept (*בהמה*) to existing particular (*הבהמה*). Grammar tracks ontology.

כל.explanation:
The word *כל (kol)* means all, every, the whole — when followed by a defined noun it means the whole; when followed by an indefinite noun it means every. BDB: *כל (kol)* — all, the whole, every; one of the most frequent words in the Hebrew Bible, appearing approximately 5,400 times. Strong's H3605.

*כל (kol)* as a free morpheme appears without any phonological attachment to the following word. When combined with a prefix *כל (kol)* + *ה (hā)* + noun, it means the whole [noun]; when combined with just כל + indefinite noun, it means every [noun]. Both patterns appear frequently in Genesis 1. *כל (kol)* is used in the climactic verse 31 — *וירא אלהים את כל אשר עשה (wayyarʾ ʾĕlōhîm ʾeṯ kol ʾăšer ʿāśāh)* — and God saw everything that he had made. The word *כל* frames the divine final evaluation.

רמש.explanation:
The noun *רמש (remeś)* means creeping things — ground-level creatures that creep, crawl, or slither. BDB: *רמש (remeś)* — creeping things; from the root *רמש (rāmaś)*, to creep. Strong's H7431. See also the entry *ורמש* above.

In verse 25, *כל רמש האדמה (kol remeś hāʾădāmāh)* — all creeping things of the ground — uses the construct *האדמה (hāʾădāmāh)* (the ground/soil) rather than *הארץ (hāʾāreṣ)* (the earth). The more specific *אדמה* (cultivable topsoil) is used here, emphasizing that the creeping creatures are associated with the topmost layer of earth — the surface soil — rather than the broader concept of earth. This attention to precision is characteristic of the creation account.

האדמה.explanation:
The noun *אדמה (ʾădāmāh)* means ground, soil, cultivable land — the productive topsoil. BDB: *אדמה (ʾădāmāh)* — ground, land, earth; specifically cultivable soil, arable land; from the same root as *אדם (ʾādām)* (human/Adam). Strong's H127 documents approximately 222 occurrences.

The etymological relationship between *אדמה (ʾădāmāh)* (ground) and *אדם (ʾādām)* (human/Adam) is one of the most theologically freighted word-pairs in the Hebrew Bible. Humans are *ʾādām*, creatures of the *ʾădāmāh*. In Genesis 2:7, God forms Adam from the dust of the *ʾădāmāh*; in Genesis 3:19, the human will return to the *ʾădāmāh* from which it was taken. The *ʾādām-ʾădāmāh* relationship (human-ground) is the anthropological foundation of biblical theology. In Genesis 1:25, the creeping things of the *ʾădāmāh* occupy the lowest ecological stratum — the same ground from which humanity will be formed.

נעשה.explanation:
The form *נעשה (naʿăśeh)* is the Qal cohortative (or imperfect) 1cp of the root *עשה (ʿāśāh)* — let us make! The 1cp form is the only first-person plural in the creation account. BDB: Qal 1cp imperfect/cohortative — let us do, we will make. Strong's H6213.

The first-person plural *נעשה (naʿăśeh)* — let us make — in verse 26 is one of the most debated phrases in Genesis. Who is God speaking to? Ancient Jewish interpreters proposed: the divine council of heavenly beings; the act of self-consultation; the earth (parallel to 'let the earth bring forth' in v.24). Christian interpreters have read it as a Trinitarian reference. Modern scholarship often sees it as the plural of self-deliberation or as God addressing the divine assembly. The text does not resolve the question. What is clear is that the creation of humanity involves a deliberation or consultation that distinguishes it from the prior commands.

אדם.explanation:
The noun *אדם (ʾādām)* means human being, man, humanity — and later becomes the proper name Adam. BDB: *אדם (ʾādām)* — man, mankind, human being; related to *אדמה (ʾădāmāh)* (ground, red earth) and *אדם (ʾāḏam)* (to be red). Strong's H120 documents approximately 562 occurrences.

In Genesis 1:26–27, *אדם (ʾādām)* appears without the definite article — not the-man but simply man/humanity. The creation of *אדם* is the creation of humanity as a category, not just one individual. In verse 27, the explicit statement that God created *הָאָדָם (hāʾādām)* (with article) — the man — as *זָכָר וּנְקֵבָה (zāḵār ûnĕqêḇāh)* (male and female) confirms that *ʾādām* encompasses the full human category, both sexes. *אדם* as humanity precedes *הָאָדָם* as the specific named individual of Genesis 2.

בצלמנו.explanation:
The form *בצלמנו (bĕṣalmênū)* is *ב (bĕ)* (in) + *צלם (ṣelem)* (image) + *נו (nū)* (our, 1cp possessive) — in our image. BDB: *צלם (ṣelem)* — image, likeness, form; the visible representation or replica of something. Strong's H6754 documents approximately 17 occurrences.

*צלם (ṣelem)* in the ancient Near East referred to a physical idol or statue — an image representing a deity or king. The king's *ṣelem* was placed throughout his empire to represent his presence and authority. When Genesis 1:26 says humans are created *בצלמנו* — in our image — it is using royal image-language: humans are placed in the world as God's representative images, indicating his sovereign presence throughout creation. The 1cp form *נו* — our — contributes to the plural of verse 26: the image corresponds to the plural subject of *נעשה* (let us make).

כדמותנו.explanation:
The form *כדמותנו (kiḏmûṯênū)* is *כ (kĕ)* (like, according to) + *דמות (dĕmût)* (likeness) + *נו (nū)* (our, 1cp possessive) — according to our likeness. BDB: *דמות (dĕmût)* — likeness, resemblance, similarity; from the root *דמה (dāmāh)* — to be like, to resemble. Strong's H1823 documents 25 occurrences.

The pairing of *צלם (ṣelem)* (image) and *דמות (dĕmût)* (likeness) in verse 26 has generated extensive theological discussion. Are these synonyms, or does each add something? Some interpreters see *ṣelem* as physical resemblance and *dĕmût* as spiritual/functional resemblance. Others see them as emphatic synonyms, the doubling intensifying the claim. BDB treats them as near-synonyms covering the range of similarity. The *כ (kĕ)* prefix — like, according to — before *דמות* may soften the claim slightly: not identical to God but formed in the pattern of God's likeness.

וירדו.explanation:
The form *וירדו (wĕyirdū)* is the Qal jussive 3mp of the root *רדה (rādāh)* — and let them have dominion. BDB: *רדה (rādāh)* — to rule, to have dominion, to exercise authority; used of kings, leaders, and here of humanity's governance of the animal world. Strong's H7287 documents approximately 25 occurrences.

*רדה (rādāh)* is royal governance language. The same root describes Solomon's rule (*1 Kings 5:4*) and the Davidic king's dominion in Psalm 72:8. When Genesis 1:26 assigns *רדה* to humanity — *וירדו (wĕyirdū)* — it installs humans as vice-regents with an explicitly royal mandate over creation. The mandate is exercised in the image of God: humans govern as God's representative rulers, not as autonomous owners. The dominion given is ecological stewardship under divine authority, not exploitation.

בדגת.explanation:
The form *בדגת (biḏgaṯ)* is *ב (bĕ)* (in/over) + *דגת (ḏagaṯ)*, the construct form of *דגה (dāgāh)* (fish, collective). BDB: *דגה (dāgāh)* — fish, collective noun for fish; construct דגת. Strong's H1710 and H1709.

The word *דג (dāg)* is the singular noun for fish; *דגה (dāgāh)* is the collective. In Hebrew, many organisms are described by collective nouns (עוף, birds collectively; דגה, fish collectively) that do not require plural marking. In Genesis 1:26–28, *בדגת הים (biḏgaṯ hayyām)* — in/over the fish of the sea — uses the construct collective. Humanity's dominion over sea creatures is the first item in the list of dominion scope: sea, sky, land. The sequence moves from the created zones established in days 1–3 to the creatures filling them in days 4–6.

הים.explanation:
The definite form *הים (hayyām)* — the sea — singular with article. BDB: *ים (yām)* — sea; singular often refers to the specific sea (Mediterranean), while plural *ימים (yammîm)* refers to seas generally. Strong's H3220.

In Genesis 1:26 and 28, *הים (hayyām)* — the sea — appears as the singular in *דגת הים (ḏagaṯ hayyām)* — fish of the sea. The singular may refer specifically to the great sea (Mediterranean) as the representative sea, or may be a generic singular. The shift between *ימים (yammîm)* (seas, plural, v.22) and *הים (hayyām)* (the sea, singular, v.26) may be stylistic variation or may reflect different conceptual scopes: blessing addresses all seas; dominion language addresses the sea as a generic category.

ובעוף.explanation:
The form *ובעוף (ûḇĕʿôp̄)* is *ו (wĕ)* (and) + *ב (bĕ)* (in/over) + *עוף (ʿôp̄)* (birds). Together: and over the birds. BDB: *עוף (ʿôp̄)*. Strong's H5775.

The preposition *ב (bĕ)* before the animal categories in verse 26 — *בדגת הים (biḏgaṯ hayyām)*, *ובעוף השמים (ûḇĕʿôp̄ haššāmayim)*, *ובבהמה (ûḇabbĕhēmāh)* — frames the dominion as over (governing within) those domains. Dominion is locative: having authority within the sphere of the sea, within the sphere of the sky, within the sphere of the land. The *ב (bĕ)* preposition creates an image of governance-within rather than oversight-from-above.

ובבהמה.explanation:
The form *ובבהמה (ûḇabbĕhēmāh)* is *ו (wĕ)* (and) + *ב (bĕ)* (over) + *בהמה (bĕhēmāh)* (livestock) with the definite article assimilating: and over the livestock. BDB: *בהמה (bĕhēmāh)* with preposition *ב (bĕ)*. Strong's H929.

The third domain of human dominion in verse 26 is the livestock — domestic animals. This extends across all three created zones: the sea (fish), the sky (birds), and the land (livestock and creeping things). The comprehensive list ensures no domain of animate life escapes the scope of human governance. The mandate is total in geographical reach but bounded by category: humans are over animals, not over other humans or over God.

ובכל.explanation:
The form *ובכל (ûḇĕḵāl)* is *ו (wĕ)* (and) + *ב (bĕ)* (over) + *כל (kol)* (all/every) — and over all. BDB: *כל (kol)* — all, every. Strong's H3605.

In verse 26, *ובכל הארץ (ûḇĕḵāl hāʾāreṣ)* — and over all the earth — and *ובכל הרמש (ûḇĕḵāl hāremeś)* — and over every creeping thing — use *ובכל* to extend the dominion comprehensively. The scope is not just some animals but all classes; not just the cultivated land but the whole earth. *כל (kol)* ensures the mandate is without geographical or categorical exception.

הרמש.explanation:
The form *הרמש (hārōmêś)* in verse 30 is the Qal active participle of *רמש (rāmaś)* with the article — the one creeping, that which creeps. In verse 30, *ולכל הרמש (ûlĕḵōl rōmêś)* — and to everything that moves — uses the participle without the article to describe the broad class. BDB: Qal participle of *רמש (rāmaś)* — creeping, moving. Strong's H7430.

The participial form *רמש/הרמש* appears in verses 21, 26, 28, and 30, describing creatures by their movement rather than their species. In verse 30, including all creeping things in the food-recipients list ensures that even the lowest stratum of land life — the creatures that move at ground level — are provided for within the Creator's economy of provision. No creature is forgotten in the food allocation.

האדם.explanation:
The definite form *האדם (hāʾādām)* — the man — with the definite article. In verse 27, *ויברא אלהים את האדם (wayyiḇrāʾ ʾĕlōhîm ʾeṯ hāʾādām)* — God created the man — uses the article to specify the human as a particular created being. In verse 26 the term was *אדם (ʾādām)* without the article (generic humanity); in verse 27 *האדם (hāʾādām)* — the human specifically — is created. BDB: *אדם (ʾādām)* with definite article. Strong's H120.

The shift from *אדם (ʾādām)* (v.26, generic) to *הָאָדָם (hāʾādām)* (v.27, specific) is grammatically precise. The divine deliberation (v.26) speaks of *ʾādām* as a category to be made; the act (v.27) creates *hāʾādām* — this specific human. Yet the verse immediately clarifies that *hāʾādām* consists of both male and female — so even the definite singular refers to humanity as a collective unit, not just one individual.

בצלמו.explanation:
The form *בצלמו (bĕṣalmōw)* is *ב (bĕ)* (in) + *צלם (ṣelem)* (image) + *ו (ô)* (3ms possessive, his) — in his image. This contrasts with *בצלמנו (bĕṣalmênū)* (in our image, v.26) by shifting to the singular 3ms possessive. BDB: *צלם (ṣelem)*. Strong's H6754.

The verse's structure in verse 27 shifts from the plural of the deliberation (in our image, v.26) to the singular of execution (in his image, v.27). God alone performs the creative act; the result is singular possession. The movement from *our* to *his* may reflect that creation is a divine act performed now by God alone, even if deliberated with others. The chiastic structure — created / in his image // in the image of God / he created — surrounds *hāʾādām* with the word image from both sides.

בצלם.explanation:
The form *בצלם (bĕṣelem)* is *ב (bĕ)* (in) + *צלם (ṣelem)* (image) without a suffix, in construct relationship with the following genitive *אלהים (ʾĕlōhîm)* — in the image of God. BDB: *צלם (ṣelem)* in construct before a genitive. Strong's H6754.

In verse 27, the three-part structure — *בצלמו (bĕṣalmōw)*, *בצלם אלהים (bĕṣelem ʾĕlōhîm)*, and *בצלמו (bĕṣalmōw)* (implied by the parallel) — creates an explicit identification: God's image, in which the human is created, is God's own image. *In his image = in the image of God*. The two phrases are appositive: the possessive and the genitive construct are two ways of saying the same thing. The human bears the divine image, and this is precisely the divine image — not some third thing but God's own visual-representational character.

אתו.explanation:
The form *אתו (ʾōṯōw)* is the direct object pronoun him — the accusative form of the 3ms pronoun, structured as *את (ʾêṯ)* (direct object marker) + *ו (ô)* (3ms possessive/pronoun). BDB: pronominal object. Strong's H853+suffix.

In verse 27, *בָּרָא אֹתֹו (bārāʾ ʾōṯōw)* — he created him — specifies the first creation as singular before the verse expands to *בָּרָא אֹתָם (bārāʾ ʾōṯām)* — he created them. The progression from singular (him) to plural (them) within a single verse tracks the internal logic of the verse: the image-bearing human being is first singular (*hāʾādām*, the man), then revealed as plural (*zāḵār ûnĕqēḇāh*, male and female). *אתו (ʾōṯōw)* and *אתם (ʾōṯām)* are the pivots of this grammatical-theological movement.

זכר.explanation:
The noun *זכר (zāḵār)* means male — the male of any species, human or animal. BDB: *זכר (zāḵār)* — male, man; from the root *זכר (zāḵar)* — to remember, to mark. Some etymologists suggest the connection is anatomical (a prominent mark); others dispute this. Strong's H2145 documents approximately 82 occurrences.

In Genesis 1:27, *זָכָר וּנְקֵבָה (zāḵār ûnĕqêḇāh)* — male and female — identifies biological sex as a feature of the created human in the image of God. Both male and female bear the divine image; neither sex is the primary image-bearer. The pairing establishes sexual differentiation as part of the original good creation, not a fall into materiality. The root *זכר* (remember) and *זכר* (male) are homophones; whether the author intended a connection between masculinity and memory is debated.

ונקבה.explanation:
The noun *נקבה (nĕqêḇāh)* means female — from the root *נקב (nāqaḇ)*, to pierce, to bore a hole. BDB: *נקבה (nĕqêḇāh)* — female; etymologically related to the verb *נקב (nāqaḇ)* — to pierce, bore. The anatomical derivation reflects an ancient understanding of biological sex difference. Strong's H5347 documents approximately 22 occurrences.

The pairing *זָכָר וּנְקֵבָה (zāḵār ûnĕqêḇāh)* — male and female — is repeated in Genesis 6:19 (Noah's ark), 7:3, and Leviticus 12:7, among other places. The binary is a created order binary: God made human beings as biologically sexually differentiated beings. The *ו (wĕ)* — and — joining *זכר* and *נקבה* is simple conjunction: male and female together constitute *הָאָדָם* (the human). Neither alone is the complete human image-bearer; together they carry the divine image.

להם.explanation:
The form *להם (lāhem)* is the preposition *ל (lĕ)* (to) + *הם (hem)* (3mp pronoun, them) — to them. BDB: preposition *ל (lĕ)* with 3mp pronominal suffix. Strong's H<a combined form>.

In verse 28, *ויאמר להם אלהים (wayyōmer lāhem ʾĕlōhîm)* — God said to them — is the first instance of God speaking directly and addressing humans in the 2nd person. This is the first divine speech addressed to human beings. *להם (lāhem)* specifies that the blessing-content (Be fruitful and multiply) is spoken directly to the humans who have just been created. The preposition *ל (lĕ)* in indirect-object position marks humans as the recipients of divine speech, establishing a dialogical relationship between Creator and creation's culminating inhabitants.

וכבשה.explanation:
The form *וכבשה (wĕḵiḇšuhā)* is *ו (wĕ)* (and) + Qal imperative 2mp of *כבש (kāḇaš)* (subdue) + *ה (hā)* (3fs object suffix, her/it) — and subdue it (the earth). BDB: *כבש (kāḇaš)* — to subdue, to bring into subjection, to tread down; used of military conquest and of bringing land under cultivation. Strong's H3533 documents approximately 15 occurrences.

*כבש (kāḇaš)* is a strong verb, suggesting forceful labor and effort. The earth does not yield its produce without work. This single word in verse 28 establishes that the human vocation involves active, effortful cultivation of the earth — not passive enjoyment of an automatic paradise. The curse of Genesis 3 (toil, thorns, sweat) intensifies this labor but does not invent it; tilling and guarding the garden was the original human vocation before the fall.

ורדו.explanation:
The form *ורדו (ûrĕḏū)* is *ו (wĕ)* (and) + Qal imperative 2mp of *רדה (rādāh)* — and have dominion! This is the direct-address imperative form of the same root used in verse 26's jussive *וירדו (wĕyirdū)* (let them rule). The deliberation uses the jussive (3rd person); the blessing directly commands with the imperative (2nd person): you, have dominion. BDB: *רדה (rādāh)* — to rule, to govern. Strong's H7287.

The shift from 3rd-person jussive (*וירדו*, and let them rule, v.26) to 2nd-person imperative (*ורדו*, and you shall rule, v.28) marks the transition from decree to command: first God decides (v.26) then God commands (v.28) directly to humanity. The dominion mandate is thus both decreed in creation's design and commanded to the creatures who receive it. Humans are not passive recipients of dominion; they are actively commissioned to exercise it.

הנה.explanation:
The particle *הנה (hinnêh)* means behold, look, here is — an attention-commanding particle that emphasizes the importance or immediacy of what follows. BDB: *הנה (hinnêh)* — behold, look; interjection calling attention; often introduces an important announcement. Strong's H2009 documents approximately 1,061 occurrences.

In Genesis 1:29, *הנה נתתי לכם (hinnêh nāṯattî lāḵem)* — behold, I have given you — the *הנה* frames the food grant as something remarkable and worth attending to. The divine gift of food is not casual; it is a formal announcement worthy of the attention-call. The same particle opens verse 31 in the different form *וְהִנֵּה (wĕhinnêh)* — and behold — when God surveys the completed creation. The particle creates dramatic pauses in the narrative: look at what I am doing; look at what has been made.

נתתי.explanation:
The form *נתתי (nāṯattî)* is the Qal perfect 1cs of the root *נתן (nāṯan)* — I have given. The 1cs perfect with *תי (tî)* suffix. BDB: *נתן (nāṯan)* — to give, to grant, to appoint. Strong's H5414.

The 1cs perfect *נתתי (nāṯattî)* — I have given — is rhetorically significant: the perfect tense marks the food grant as already accomplished, a completed divine act that the speech announces. God does not say I will give; he says I have given. The food of the created world is already granted — it belongs to humans and creatures by divine prior decision. The speech in verse 29 announces what has already been established in creation. This is the performative function of the perfect: the act is so certain it is declared as already complete.

לכם.explanation:
The form *לכם (lāḵem)* is the preposition *ל (lĕ)* (to/for) + *כם (ḵem)* (2mp suffix, you all) — to you (plural). This is the 2nd masculine plural indirect object pronoun. BDB: preposition *ל (lĕ)* with 2mp pronominal suffix. Strong's H<combined form>.

In verse 29, *לכם (lāḵem)* addresses the human pair (male and female, v.27) as the recipients of the food grant. The 2mp (plural) address confirms that the food grant is given to humanity collectively, not to one individual. The same form appears twice in verse 29 — *לכם (lāḵem)* (I have given to you) and again at the end (to you it shall be for food) — framing the food grant statement with its recipients. The food is for you, specifically and intentionally.

יהיה.explanation:
The form *יהיה (yihyeh)* is the Qal imperfect 3ms of the root *היה (hāyāh)* — it will be / it shall be. BDB: Qal imperfect — a future or modal form: it will be, let it be. Strong's H1961.

In verse 29, *לכם יהיה לאכלה (lāḵem yihyeh lĕʾāḵlāh)* — to you it shall be for food — the imperfect *יהיה* functions as a modal: the plants are designated to serve as your food. The *ל (lĕ)* before *אכלה (ʾāḵlāh)* (food) is the *ל* of purpose: they shall be *for* food. The food grant is not just acknowledged; it is assigned a purpose. The created plants exist *for* human and animal consumption. Creation is purposive at every level, including this dietary level.

לאכלה.explanation:
The form *לאכלה (lĕʾāḵlāh)* is *ל (lĕ)* (for) + *אכלה (ʾāḵlāh)* (food, eating). BDB: *אכלה (ʾāḵlāh)* — food, nourishment; from the root *אכל (ʾāḵal)* — to eat. Strong's H402 and H399.

The noun *אכלה (ʾāḵlāh)* is a verbal noun from *אכל (ʾāḵal)* (to eat), meaning what is eaten, i.e., food. The *ה (āh)* suffix forms an abstract/activity noun from the verbal root. In verses 29–30, *לאכלה (lĕʾāḵlāh)* closes both the human food grant and the animal food grant. The identical phrase at the close of both — *לכם יהיה לאכלה (lāḵem yihyeh lĕʾāḵlāh)* (to you for food) and *לאכלה (lĕʾāḵlāh)* — creates a rhetorical inclusio. The two food allocations are formally parallel, structurally complete, and closed by the same word.

ולכל.gloss: and to all, and to every
ולכל.explanation:
The form *ולכל (ûlĕḵōl)* is *ו (wĕ)* — and — + *ל (lĕ)* — to — + *כל (kol)* — all, every. Together: and to all, and to every. BDB: *כל (kol)* — all, every. Strong's H3605.

Appearing twice in verse 20, *ûlĕḵōl* extends the naming scope to cover all categories: and to all livestock (lĕḵāl), and to the birds (ûlĕʾôp̄ haššāmāyim), and to all field beasts (*ûlĕḵōl ḥayyat haśśāḏeh*). The repeated *ûlĕḵōl* with different noun categories creates a comprehensive list construction: all + category-1, all + category-2, all + category-3. This is the Hebrew equivalent of 'all animals everywhere.' The naming covers the complete animal world of Genesis 1:24–25 (land animals: livestock, ground creepers, wild beasts) plus the birds of 1:20–21.

עוף.explanation: The unaffixed form *עוף (ʿôp̄)* without any prefix or article — bird. This occurs in verses 21 and 30 where לכל עוף (to every bird) and כל עוף (every bird) appear. BDB: *עוף (ʿôp̄)* — bird, collective and individual. Strong's H5775. See the entry for *ועוף* for the fuller explanation of this root.

ירק.explanation:
The adjective *ירק (yereq)* means green — specifically the color green as associated with vegetation. BDB: *ירק (yereq)* — green, yellow-green; used of plants, herbs, and vegetation. Strong's H3418 documents approximately 8 occurrences.

In verse 30, *כל ירק עשב (kol yereq ʿēśeḇ)* — every green herb — the adjective *ירק* specifies the green, living plant matter given to animals as food. The distinction from fruit trees and seed-bearing plants (given to humans in v.29) is made by the adjective: animals receive the green vegetation — leaves, grasses — rather than the fruit-and-seed-bearing plants. The dietary distinction is taxonomic and precise, embedded in the vocabulary of creation.

והנה.explanation:
The form *והנה (wĕhinnêh)* is *ו (wĕ)* (and) + *הנה (hinnêh)* (behold) — and behold. BDB: *הנה (hinnêh)* with conjunctive *ו (wĕ)*. Strong's H2009+H2050.

In verse 31, *וְהִנֵּה טוֹב מְאֹד (wĕhinnêh ṭôḇ mĕʾōḏ)* — and behold, it was very good — the attention particle introduces the climactic divine evaluation. The *ו (wĕ)* connecting it to the preceding clause (*and God saw everything he had made*) makes the evaluation a result of the seeing: God looked, and here now — behold — it was very good. *והנה* amplifies the evaluation: this is not merely a quiet noting but an emphatic declaration requiring the reader's attention.

מאד.explanation:
The adverb *מאד (mĕʾōḏ)* means very, greatly, exceedingly — intensifying whatever it modifies. BDB: *מאד (mĕʾōḏ)* — exceedingly, very, much; an intensifying adverb. Strong's H3966 documents approximately 300 occurrences.

In Genesis 1:31, *טוֹב מְאֹד (ṭôḇ mĕʾōḏ)* — very good — elevates the final evaluation above all the preceding *כי טוב (kî ṭôḇ)* evaluations. The completed whole is not merely good but very good. This is the only evaluation in the chapter where *מאד* appears. The adverb is rare here — its first and only occurrence in the chapter — which gives it maximum rhetorical weight. The creation, evaluated piece by piece as good, is now evaluated as a total as exceeding good.

הששי.explanation:
The ordinal *הששי (haššiššî)* — the sixth — with the definite article. BDB: *ששי (šiššî)* — sixth (ordinal); from the root *שש (šēš)* (six). Strong's H8337. Day six is uniquely designated with the definite article (*ה*), while days two through five use ordinals without the article (*שני, שלישי, רביעי, חמישי*). Day one uses the cardinal *אחד* without article.

The presence of the definite article on *הששי (haššiššî)* — the sixth — is a literary signal that this day is in a special relationship with what follows. Day six is *the* sixth, the specific day that completes the creation work, whose uniqueness is marked grammatically. This anticipates the equally unique *השביעי (haššĕḇîʿî)* (the seventh) in Genesis 2:2–3, where the Sabbath also bears the definite article. Together, *the* sixth and *the* seventh are paired as the culminating days: creation and rest.

ויכלו.explanation:
The form *ויכלו (wayyḵullū)* is a Pual (passive intensive) Consecutive Imperfect 3mp: and they were completed/finished. The root *כלה (kālāh)* means to complete, finish, bring to an end. BDB: to be finished, completed, spent. Strong's H3615.

The Pual stem (passive of the intensive Piel) signals that the heavens and earth were brought to completion — a completion actively done to them by God. This is not mere ending but achieved wholeness. The verbal form opens Genesis 2 as a summary title for the completed creation of Genesis 1. The same root appears in wayyiḵal (v.2, Piel active: and God finished his work), creating an opening bracket: the creation was finished (Pual passive, v.1) / God actively finished it (Piel active, v.2).

ויכל.explanation:
The form *ויכל (wayyḵal)* is a Piel Consecutive Imperfect 3ms: and he completed/finished. The Piel of *כלה (kālāh)* is intensive active — God actively brought his work to full completion. BDB: Piel — to bring to an end, to complete, to finish. Strong's H3615.

In Genesis 2:2, God finishes his work on the seventh day. The Piel completion here is paired with the Pual completion of verse 1, creating a passive-active frame: the creation was finished (v.1) // God finished it (v.2). Jewish tradition has wrestled with the apparent statement that God finished on the seventh day — if he finished on the seventh, did he still work on the seventh? Rashi argues the rest itself was the final completion: the act of resting was what made the creation fully finished.

וישבת.explanation:
The form *וישבת (wayyišbōṯ)* is a Qal Consecutive Imperfect 3ms of *שבת (šāḇaṯ)*: and he rested/ceased. The root *שבת* means primarily to cease, to stop — not to recuperate or sleep. BDB: to cease, stop, rest. Strong's H7673.

The root *שבת* gives us the noun *שבת (šabbāṯ)* — Sabbath, the weekly day of cessation. God's resting on the seventh day is the theological foundation of Israel's Sabbath practice. The Decalogue explicitly grounds the Sabbath commandment in this creation narrative (Exodus 20:11). What God does on the creation Sabbath — cease from work — is what Israel is commanded to do every seventh day. The Sabbath is a commemoration of God's own creative rhythm built into the fabric of the week.

השביעי.explanation:
The form *השביעי (haššĕḇîʿî)* means the seventh, from the root *שבע (šeḇaʿ)* — seven. BDB: *שביעי (šĕḇîʿî)* — seventh; ordinal form of seven. Strong's H7637.

The number seven is the organizing number of the Hebrew Bible's priestly world: seven days, seven weeks, seven years, the Year of Jubilee (7×7+1), the menorah's seven lamps, the seven holy days of the festival calendar. *השביעי* appears three times in Genesis 2:2–3, emphasizing the seventh day's unique status. The definite article (the seventh, not simply seventh) matches the unique article on *הששי* (the sixth) in 1:31, marking these as paired, elevated days at the culmination of the creation week.

מלאכתו.explanation:
The form *מלאכתו (mĕlaḵtōw)* is the noun *מלאכה (mĕlāḵāh)* — work, occupation, craftsmanship — with a 3ms possessive suffix: his work. BDB: *מלאכה* — work, occupation, business, task. Strong's H4399.

The root *מלאכה* is closely related to the root that gives *מלאך (malʾāḵ)* — messenger, angel, agent. Work is what an agent (malʾāḵ) does on behalf of a sender. God's creative mĕlāḵāh is the work done as divine sovereign. The word appears in the Sabbath commandment (Exodus 20:9–10): *six days you shall labor and do all your mĕlāḵāh...on the seventh day you shall do no mĕlāḵāh*. The Sabbath prohibition uses the same word that describes God's own creative activity in Genesis 2:2–3.

מכל.explanation:
The form *מכל (mikkāl)* is the preposition *מן (min)* — from, out of — assimilated and attached to *כל (kol)* — all, every, whole. Combined: from all, from the whole of. BDB: *כל (kol)* — the whole, all, every. Strong's H3605.

*מכל (mikkāl)* appears in Genesis 2:2–3 twice (from all his work), establishing that God's cessation is total and comprehensive — not from some of his work but from all of it. The phrase *mik·kāl mĕlaḵtô* (from all his work) creates a formula of completeness: the creation is entirely done. The same construction — *mikkōl* + noun — appears throughout Genesis in the garden prohibition (from all the trees of the garden — 2:16) and in the food allocations (from all green plants — 1:30).

ויקדש.explanation:
The form *ויקדש (wayyĕqaddêš)* is a Piel Consecutive Imperfect 3ms: and he sanctified/made holy. The root *קדש (qāḏaš)* means to be set apart, holy, consecrated. BDB: Piel — to consecrate, to sanctify, to set apart as holy. Strong's H6942.

This is the first occurrence of the root *קדש (qāḏaš)* in the entire Bible. Before any place (tabernacle, Sinai) or person (priest, Nazirite) is declared holy, time is declared holy. The seventh day is the Bible's first sacred thing. Holiness is not first a spatial concept (holy ground) but a temporal one (holy day). The Sabbath is the first sanctuary — a sanctuary in time, not space. This temporal holiness becomes the pattern and prototype for all subsequent holiness in the Hebrew Bible.

לעשות.explanation:
The form *לעשות (laʿăśôṯ)* is the Qal Infinitive Construct with the preposition *ל (lĕ)* — for/to do, to make. BDB: Qal infinitive of *עשה (ʿāśāh)* — to do, make. Strong's H6213.

In Genesis 2:3, the phrase *ʾăšer-bārāʾ ʾĕlōhîm laʿăśôṯ* — which God created to do — is an unusual construction. The Infinitive *laʿăśôṯ* follows *bārāʾ*, suggesting the creation was itself purposeful: God created the world as ongoing work, as something to continue to be done. Some interpreters read this as the creation was begun with the intent of future cultivation and completion by humans. The sentence embeds human vocation — to work, to do — within the act of divine creation.

יהוה.explanation:
The tetragrammaton *יהוה (YHWH)* — the LORD — is the personal covenant name of God. BDB: the proper name of the God of Israel, related to the root *היה (hāyāh)* — to be. Strong's H3068.

*יהוה* appears for the first time in the Bible at Genesis 2:4, always in combination with *אלהים (ʾĕlōhîm)* as *יהוה אלהים (YHWH ʾĕlōhîm)* — the LORD God. This compound divine name dominates the garden narrative (Genesis 2–3) and distinguishes its authorial strand from the ʾĕlōhîm of Genesis 1. YHWH is the name revealed to Moses at the burning bush (Exodus 3:14–15) as *ʾehyeh ʾăšer ʾehyeh* — I AM WHO I AM. The name emphasizes the personal, relational, eternal being of God. Jewish tradition does not pronounce YHWH but substitutes *Adonai* (Lord). English translations follow this by rendering YHWH as LORD (in small capitals).

תולדות.gloss: the generations of, the account of
תולדות.explanation:
The form *תולדות (tôlĕḏôṯ)* is a feminine plural noun derived from the root *ילד (yālaḏ)* — to bear, to beget. Literally it means begatings, births, but functionally it serves as the technical term for the section-heading formula of Genesis. BDB: descendants, generations, family history, account. Strong's H8435.

*תולדות* appears 10 times in Genesis as a structural formula: these are the generations/account of X. It introduces: the heavens and earth (2:4), Adam (5:1), Noah (6:9), Shem, Ham, and Japheth (10:1, called the Table of Nations), Shem (11:10), Terah (11:27, introducing Abraham's story), Ishmael (25:12), Isaac (25:19), Esau (36:1, 9), and Jacob (37:2, introducing the Joseph story). The formula organizes Genesis into 10 major sections, each centered on a named party whose descendants or story follows.

בהבראם.explanation:
The form *בהבראם (bĕhibbārĕʾām)* is the preposition *ב (bĕ)* + Niphal Infinitive Construct of *ברא (bārāʾ)* + 3mp suffix: in their being created. The Niphal is the passive stem. BDB: Niphal of *ברא* — to be created. Strong's H1254.

Rashi famously noted that the consonants *בהבראם (bĕhibbārĕʾām)* are an anagram of *בְּאַבְרָהָם (bĕʾaḇrāhām)* — with/for Abraham. This reading, while midrashic, points to the connection between creation and the covenant: the heavens and earth were created for the sake of Abraham and his descendants. In the plain meaning, the phrase simply means when they were created — a passive reference back to the creation of Genesis 1, now reprised as the title-formula of Genesis 2.

עשות.gloss: to make, on the day of making
עשות.explanation:
The form *עשות (ʿăśôṯ)* is the Qal Infinitive Construct of *עשה (ʿāśāh)* — to make, to do — without a prefixed preposition attached. In Genesis 2:4 it appears in the phrase *bĕyôm ʿăśôṯ YHWH ʾĕlōhîm ʾereṣ wĕšāmāyim* — on the day of YHWH God's making of earth and heaven. The infinitive is in construct state, taking *YHWH ʾĕlōhîm* as its subject. BDB: Qal infinitive construct of *עשה*. Strong's H6213.

The phrase *bĕyôm ʿăśôṯ* — on the day of making — uses *yôm (day)* in a non-literal temporal sense: at the time, when. This matches the usage of *yôm* throughout the ancient Near East as a period of time rather than a literal 24-hour day. Genesis 2:4b uses *day* to encompass the whole creation event, not a specific 24-hour period.

ושמים.explanation:
The form *ושמים (wĕšāmāyim)* is *ו (wĕ)* — and — + *שמים (šāmāyim)* — heavens — without the definite article. BDB: *שמים (šāmayim)* — heaven, sky. Strong's H8064.

In verse 4, the order of heavens and earth is reversed from verse 1: Genesis 1:1 says *the heavens and the earth* (heavens first); Genesis 2:4b says *earth and heavens* (earth first). This reversal is often cited as a literary marker: Genesis 2:4b begins a new section with an earthward orientation — the narrative of chapter 2 focuses on the ground (*hāʾădāmāh*) and its human cultivator, not the cosmic sky. The heavens come second because this chapter is primarily an earth story.

אלה.explanation:
The demonstrative pronoun *אלה (ʾēlleh)* means these — the plural demonstrative for near objects. BDB: demonstrative pronoun, plural — these. Strong's H428.

*אלה (ʾēlleh)* begins the *tôlĕḏôṯ* formula: *ʾēlleh tôlĕḏôṯ haššāmayim wĕhāʾāreṣ* — these are the generations of the heavens and the earth. The demonstrative *these* points forward to what follows (the garden narrative) as much as it summarizes what precedes (the creation of Genesis 1). The formula is both backward-looking (what has already been created) and forward-looking (what will now be narrated). It serves as a connecting hinge between chapters 1 and 2.

שיח.explanation:
The noun *שיח (śîaḥ)* means a bush, shrub, or wild plant — specifically uncultivated desert scrub vegetation. BDB: bush, shrub — wild undomesticated plant growth. Strong's H7880.

*שיח* is rare, appearing only twice in the Hebrew Bible: here in Genesis 2:5 and in Genesis 21:15, where Hagar places her dying son under a *śîaḥ* after being cast out into the wilderness of Beersheba. Both occurrences situate *śîaḥ* in contexts of wilderness and deprivation. In Genesis 2:5, its absence — no bush of the field yet existed — marks the pre-human, pre-rain world as incompletely formed. The landscape waits for the conditions (rain from above, human from below) that will allow vegetation to thrive. *śîaḥ haśśādeh* (bush of the field) is specifically the wild, uncultivated variety distinct from the garden's cultivated trees.

השדה.explanation:
The form *השדה (haśśāḏeh)* is *ה (ha)* — the definite article — + *שדה (śāḏeh)* — field, open country. BDB: field, open land, countryside — the unenclosed land outside settlements and cultivated gardens. Strong's H7704.

*שדה (śāḏeh)* appears frequently in Genesis 2–4, contrasting with the enclosed *גן (gan)* — garden. The field is open, wild, agricultural land. Cain tills the ground (becoming a farmer); Abel keeps flocks in the open field. The serpent is described as a creature of the field (3:1). The field is where human vocation meets the untamed earth — neither the edenic garden sanctuary nor wilderness, but the contested cultivated land between them. *שיח השדה (śîaḥ haśśādeh)* — bush of the field — designates the wild scrub of uncultivated country.

טרם.explanation:
The adverb *טרם (ṭerem)* means not yet, before, prior to — marking temporal priority: something has not yet happened. BDB: not yet. Strong's H2962.

*טרם* in Genesis 2:5 creates a before-state: before rain, before human, before vegetation. The word identifies the pre-creation conditions that must be met before the garden world was complete. The narrative strategy of verse 5 — describing what was NOT yet present — is the negative space that makes the positive narrative of verse 7 (and God formed the human) narratively necessary. *ṭerem* appears in legal contexts (before a court convenes, before an event), in historical contexts (before the flood, before the exodus), and here in cosmological contexts: before the conditions of ordered life were established.

יצמח.explanation:
The form *יצמח (yiṣmāḥ)* is a Qal Imperfect 3ms of *צמח (ṣāmaḥ)* — to spring up, sprout, grow. BDB: Qal — to sprout, spring up, grow — used of plants, vegetation, and (metaphorically) the growth of the Davidic branch. Strong's H6779.

In Genesis 2:5, the Qal Imperfect is used in a clause modified by *ṭerem* (not yet), giving the sense: No plant of the field had yet sprung up. The same root appears in verse 9 as *wayyaṣmaḥ (ויצמח)* — Hiphil Consecutive Imperfect — when God causes the trees to spring up in the garden. The root *צמח* also becomes a messianic title in the prophets: the Branch (*ṣemaḥ*) of David — the legitimate royal shoot that will grow from the severed stump of David's dynasty (Isaiah 4:2; Jeremiah 23:5; Zechariah 3:8; 6:12). Creation's sprouting and messianic restoration share the same Hebrew root.

המטיר.explanation:
The form *המטיר (himṭîr)* is a Hiphil Perfect 3ms of *מטר (māṭar)* — to rain. The Hiphil is causative: to cause to rain, to send rain. BDB: Hiphil of *מטר* — to cause to rain, to rain down. Strong's H4305.

YHWH God is the undisputed cause of rain in the Hebrew Bible. The Hiphil *himṭîr* makes this causation grammatically explicit: God had not caused it to rain. The same Hiphil appears in Genesis 7:4 (I will cause it to rain for 40 days and nights — the flood) and Genesis 19:24 (YHWH rained fire and sulfur on Sodom). Rain is never accidental or automatic; it is always a divine act. This theology underlies Deuteronomy's covenantal blessings and curses based on rain: obedience brings rain (11:14); disobedience brings drought (11:17). Rain is a covenant indicator.

ואדם.explanation:
The form *ואדם (wĕʾāḏām)* is *ו (wĕ)* — and — + *אדם (ʾāḏām)* without the definite article. BDB: *אדם (ʾāḏām)* — man, human being; collectively, humankind; as a personal name, Adam. Strong's H120.

In Genesis 2:5, *ʾāḏām* without the article may be functioning as an indefinite common noun: and there was no human. The same word later in the chapter alternates between *hāʾāḏām (the human, with article) and ʾāḏām without article. The word's etymology connects it to *ʾădāmāh (ground/soil)* — human and ground share the same root. The human (ʾāḏām) is the ground-being (ʾădāmāh), and the ground needs the human to work it. Absence of rain and absence of human together explain absence of vegetation — a double dependency embedded in one verse.

אין.explanation:
The particle *אין (ʾayin)* means no, nothing, there is/was no — a negation of existence. BDB: particle of non-existence — there is/was not, no. Strong's H369.

*אין* is one of the most common negative existential particles in Biblical Hebrew. It negates the existence of something: there was no human (*wĕʾāḏām ʾayin*). In Genesis 2:5, it pairs with the preceding *ṭerem* (not yet) to create a comprehensive before-state: rain was not yet, human was not. The same word appears in the famous declaration of God's incomparability: *there is no one like YHWH our God* (Exodus 8:6). *אין* is also used of the void and nothingness from which God creates — *tohu wabohu* (Genesis 1:2) is sometimes read through the lens of *ayin*, the not-yet-existent, though the texts do not explicitly connect them.

לעבד.explanation:
The form *לעבד (laʿăḇōḏ)* is the preposition *ל (lĕ)* + Qal Infinitive Construct of *עבד (ʿāḇaḏ)* — to work, to serve, to till. BDB: to work, to serve, to till — labor on behalf of something or someone. Strong's H5647.

The root *עבד* encompasses both agricultural labor (to work the ground) and serving/worshipping (to serve God). The noun *עבד (ʿeḇeḏ)* means servant or slave. The related noun *עבודה (ʿăḇôḏāh)* means service, worship, or work — the same word used for the priestly service in the tabernacle. In Genesis 2:5 and 15, working the ground (laʿăḇōḏ ʾeṯ hāʾădāmāh) is the first human vocation. This vocation is simultaneously agricultural (tilling) and priestly (serving) — the garden is a sanctuary and the human's work in it is worship.

ואד.explanation:
The noun *אד (ʾêḏ)* means mist, stream, or underground water source. BDB: mist, stream — meaning uncertain; either mist rising from ground or subterranean channel. Strong's H108. The word is rare, appearing only here (Genesis 2:6) and in Job 36:27 (*ʾêḏ*: the water droplets/misty vapor).

Ancient versions translated the word variously: LXX rendered it *pēgē* (spring, source); Vulgate translated it *fons* (spring/fountain). Modern scholarship, influenced by Akkadian cognates (*edu*, a channel or underground watercourse), increasingly translates it as a subterranean stream or artesian spring. The *ʾêḏ* waters the whole face of the ground from below, in contrast with the rain that comes from above (which had not yet fallen, v.5). The pre-rain world had a different irrigation system: bottom-up instead of top-down.

יעלה.explanation:
